content.value: Builds the sorted array one element at a time, taking each new element and inserting it into its correct position among the already-sorted elements to its left. Efficient for small or nearly-sorted data.

Repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion and swaps it into its correct position at the front. Always O(n^2) comparisons regardless of input order, but makes at most n swaps - useful when writes are expensive.

A non-comparison sort. Counts how many times each value occurs, then uses those counts to place elements directly into output position. Runs in O(n+k) time where k is the range of input values, but only works well when k is not much larger than n (e.g. sorting exam scores 0-100).

Sorts integers digit by digit, from least significant to most significant, using a stable sort (typically counting sort) as a subroutine at each digit position. Runs in O(d*(n+k)) where d is the number of digits - excellent for sorting large lists of fixed-width integers.
Counting sort must be stable (preserve relative order of equal elements) for radix sort to work correctly - this is why the inner loop walks backward (from n-1 down to 0).

items: Insertion sort: O(n^2) worst, O(n) best (nearly sorted input)
Radix sort: O(d*(n+k)), great for large integer datasets

quiz.question: Which sort always performs O(n^2) comparisons regardless of input order?
Counting sort works best when:
options: The range of values (k) is small relative to n
The data is already a linked list
The array is huge and the value range is huge too

quiz.question: Radix sort processes digits in what order?
options: Most significant to least significant only
